segments.title: Source reliability and why citations matter
segments.summary: The opening method lesson explains why a literature review needs the right amount of relevant information and sources readers can trust.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames source choice as a practical research skill rather than a formality. The section distinguishes useful subject sources from entertaining but unsuitable references, then explains why citations let a lecturer see both the reading list and independent research behind an argument. It is a useful entry point for viewers who want a concise reminder that evidence has to be relevant, reliable, and visible.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces content balance and asks whether the information has been sourced from reliable tools.
The section contrasts a useful source within its own subject area with an unsuitable source for real-world engineering history.
Dr. Clarke explains that references show what tools the student used and whether they are moving into their own research.

segments.title: Presentation polish affects how work is read
segments.summary: A writing-practice aside argues that clean formatting can make the same content easier for a marker to read generously.
segments.body: This watch point is about the human side of assessment. Dr. Clarke argues that aligned, structured, professional-looking work encourages a marker to look for what the paper does well, while messy presentation pushes attention toward what may be missing. The practical lesson is modest but important: formatting cannot replace substance, yet poor presentation can make good research harder to reward.

evidence.note: The transcript says professional presentation can affect marks even when the underlying content is the same.
Dr. Clarke describes how a neat paper can make a marker look for credit while untidy structure can make problems stand out.

segments.title: Style, ethics, and corroboration
segments.summary: The middle of the guide links readable prose, ethical practice, and multiple sources as parts of credible research writing.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke gives three connected checks for stronger writing. Reading work aloud catches awkward prose that silent reading can miss, ethical judgment keeps procedure aligned with what is right, and corroboration tests whether several sources support the same point. The segment is especially useful for viewers thinking about how public or academic history moves beyond a single attractive source.

evidence.note: The transcript recommends reading work aloud because hearing it exposes poor style.
Dr. Clarke distinguishes best practice from the ethically correct thing to do.
The section explains corroboration through multiple similar sources, consensus, outliers, and critical thinking.

segments.title: Plan around the actual question
segments.summary: The planning section explains why a strong essay still fails if it answers an invented question or buries analysis under facts.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats planning as the guardrail that keeps research relevant. He warns that a beautifully written answer can still miss the assignment, then recommends starting with the question, making a rough plan, finding a key text, and building a better plan before gathering more sources. The later marking example makes the point concrete: evidence matters, but analysis and critical thinking usually carry more of the reward.

evidence.note: The transcript warns against answering a different question, even with strong research and writing.
Dr. Clarke recommends making a rough plan, finding a key text, consulting support, and then gathering sources around the plan.
The section contrasts too little research with fact-list overloading and says analysis usually earns more marks than evidence alone.

segments.title: SEAL paragraphs and the literature review starter formula
segments.summary: The final method lesson turns critical literature review into a repeatable structure built from paragraph logic and source comparison.
segments.body: This is the transcript's most concrete writing method. Dr. Clarke explains the SEAL structure as statement, evidence, analysis, and link, then uses it to show how each paragraph should serve both the argument and the question. He then offers a starter formula for a critical literature review: identify key areas, compare journal articles, textbooks, and web material, and summarize how those sources fit the broader question.

evidence.note: The transcript defines SEAL as statement, evidence, analysis, and link, then asks how evidence relates to both the argument and the question.
Dr. Clarke defines a critical literature review as showing broad understanding, consideration of multiple sources, and critical thinking.
The section lays out the starter formula: key areas, journal article, textbook, web article, comparison, contrast, and reliability differences.
